title: Apply: ecosystem dynamics
description: Apply your understanding of ecosystem dynamics in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from an ocean ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the otter population gets smaller?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from an ocean ecosystem. The food web shows that sea urchins consume kelp, otters consume sea urchins, zooplankton consume phytoplankton, kelp bass (fish) consume kelp and zooplankton, and bacteria decompose otters, kelp bass, and zooplankton.

choices.content: The sea urchin population will get larger.
The kelp population will get larger.
The kelp bass population will get larger.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from a forest ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the black bear population gets smaller?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from a forest ecosystem. The food web shows that swallowtail caterpillars consume persimmon trees, gray foxes consume swallowtail caterpillars, beavers consume silver maple trees, black bears consume persimmon trees and beavers, and bolete fungus decompose gray foxes and black bears.

choices.content: The beaver population will get larger.
The silver maple population will get larger.
The swallowtail caterpillar population will get smaller.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from a tundra ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the short-tailed weasel population gets smaller?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from a tundra ecosystem. The food web shows that brown lemmings consume bear sedge plants and bilberry plants, short-tailed weasels consume brown lemmings, snowy owls consume short-tailed weasels, arctic foxes consume bilberry plants and brown lemmings, and mushrooms decompose arctic foxes and snowy owls.

choices.content: The brown lemming population will get larger.
The snowy owl population will get larger.
The bear sedge population will get larger.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from a coral reef ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the triggerfish population gets larger?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from a coral reef ecosystem. The food web shows that damselfish consume phytoplankton, triggerfish consume damselfish and algae, reef sharks consume damselfish and triggerfish, convict tang fish consume algae, and bacteria decompose reef sharks and convict tang fish.

choices.content: The damselfish population will get smaller.
The algae population will get larger.
The convict tang population will get larger.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from a desert ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the kit fox population gets larger?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from a desert ecosystem. The food web shows that coyotes consume cactus plants and kit foxes, desert woodrats consume cactus plants, desert iguanas consume creosote bushes, kit foxes consume desert iguanas and desert woodrats, and fungus decomposes coyotes and kit foxes.

choices.content: The desert iguana population will get smaller.
The creosote bush population will get smaller.
The desert woodrat population will get larger.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following food web is from a freshwater lake ecosystem.

[[☃ image 1]]

**On a short timescale, what is most likely to happen if the water flea population gets larger?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A food web from a freshwater lake ecosystem. The food web shows that water fleas consume green algae, rotifers consume green algae, shiners (fish) consume water fleas, copepods consume golden algae and rotifers, and bacteria decompose shiners (fish), water fleas, and copepods.

choices.content: The green algae population will get smaller.
The shiner population will get smaller.
The rotifer population will get larger.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the size of a bird population over time.

[[☃ image 1]]

One year, the amount of rainfall in the birds' ecosystem was much higher than normal. This led to an increase in the amount of seeds available for the birds to eat.

**Which of these graphs shows how the bird population likely changed as a result of the increased rain?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: After the increased rain, there were more seeds for the birds to eat.
With more food, the birds were more likely to survive and reproduce. So, the bird population likely got bigger.

options.alt: A graph shows a line that represents bird population size over time. The line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. The line goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady.

choices.content: ![A graph shows bird population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before increased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after increased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and increases sharply, to about two-thirds the way up the y-axis. The line then continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ddd8e1781358b953fc3ad265ab51bd1f1bf86f9b)

![A graph shows bird population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before increased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after increased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and continues in the same pattern, going up and down a little bit but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f8a4ba54cca1fecb064a1abecf4877bf92d44ebc)

![A graph shows bird population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before increased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after increased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and decreases sharply, coming close to the bottom of the graph. Then, the line continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b6da0fd5992e9e495dc92463b1fe68838a6db342)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the size of a deer population over time.

[[☃ image 1]]

One year, the amount of rainfall in the deers' ecosystem was much lower than normal. This led to a decrease in the number of plants available for the deer to eat.

**Which of these graphs shows how the deer population likely changed as a result of the decreased rain?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: After the decreased rain, there were fewer plants for the deer to eat.
With less food, the deer were less likely to survive and reproduce. So, the deer population likely got smaller.

options.alt: A graph shows a line that represents deer population size over time. The line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. The line goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady.

choices.content: ![A graph shows deer population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before decreased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after decreased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and decreases sharply, coming close to the bottom of the graph. The line then continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c59351bc6b25572807597b6ff4603ef02db5764c)

![A graph shows deer population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before decreased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after decreased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and continues in the same pattern, going up and down a little bit but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a1c6c5bd91465fa196d752303be8106eac2a651b)

![A graph shows deer population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before decreased rain. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after decreased rain. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and increases sharply, to about two-thirds the way up the y-axis. The line then continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/185df6fdda9477b5c3e02afa4cc37a1cdcb6aed0)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the size of a squirrel population over time

[[☃ image 1]]

One year, a forest fire burned part of the squirrels' habitat. This led to a decrease in the amount of food and shelter available to the squirrels.

**Which of these graphs shows how the squirrel population likely changed as a result of the forest fire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: After the fire, there was less food and shelter for the squirrels.
With less food and shelter, the squirrels in the population were less likely to survive and reproduce. So, the squirrel population likely got smaller.

options.alt: A graph shows a line that represents squirrel population size over time. The line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. The line goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady.

choices.content: ![A graph shows squirrel population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before the fire. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after the fire. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and decreases sharply, coming close to the bottom of the graph. The line then continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ff8073e64d27e2d5396dbdb165804ef01f3f406)

![A graph shows squirrel population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before the fire. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after the fire. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and continues in the same pattern, going up and down a little bit but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9b03a6122eb18e32297493b399c8de2cd9b10fdc)

![A graph shows squirrel population size over time. The graph has a solid line representing time before the fire. The line becomes dashed to indicate time after the fire. The solid line begins about a third of the way up the y-axis and goes to the right. It goes up and down a little bit, but stays mostly steady. The line then becomes dashed and increases sharply, to about two-thirds the way up the y-axis. The line then continues to the right, going up and down a little bit, but staying mostly steady.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/680c3d132f5b6292e243ee0a35c642963e92203e)

assessment_items.question_content:
Lionfish are predators that began living in the Atlantic Ocean in the 1980s. This likely occurred because aquarium owners let pet lionfish go into the wild. 

[[☃ image 1]]

In the Atlantic, lionfish have no predators. They have large appetites, and eat many species of small fish. 

**Which of these is a likely result of lionfish living in the Atlantic Ocean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: "[Common lion fish Pterois volitans](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Common_lion_fish_Pterois_volitans.jpg)" by Michael Gäbler, [CC BY 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/deed.en)

choices.content: a decrease in populations of small fish
an increase in lionfish predator populations
an increase in food for other predators of small fish

assessment_items.question_content:
Burmese pythons are predators that began living in the Florida Everglades in the 1970s. This likely occurred because people let their pet pythons go into the wild.

[[☃ image 1]]

In the Everglades, these pythons have few predators. They eat many native species, including deer, small mammals, and birds. These pythons eat many of the same prey animals as other snakes in the Everglades.

**Which of these is a likely result of Burmese pythons living in the Florida Everglades?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: "[Burmese python](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burmese_python_(6887388927).jpg)" by U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service Headquarters, [CC BY 2.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/deed.en)

choices.content: a decrease in populations of native mammals
an increase in deer populations
an increase in foods for other snakes

assessment_items.question_content:
Mongoose are predators that began living on the Hawaiian Islands in the 1800s. The mongoose were brought to Hawai'i to help control rats on sugarcane farms. 

[[☃ image 1]]

In Hawai'i, mongoose have no natural predators. They eat many species of native birds and lizards.

**Which of these is a likely result of mongoose living on the Hawaiian Islands?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: a decrease in native bird populations
an increase in mongoose predator populations
an increase in food for other predators of birds
